title: Monetary Policy
description: Central bank tools, interest rates, money supply

questions.statement: When the Central Bank raises the discount rate, commercial banks are encouraged to borrow more money.
questions.payload: Raising the discount rate makes borrowing more expensive, discouraging commercial banks.

questions.statement: Open market operations involve the Central Bank buying and selling government securities.
questions.payload: Correct. OMOs are the Central Bank's primary tool for adjusting the money supply through buying and selling bonds.

questions.statement: Selling government securities by the Central Bank increases the money supply.
questions.payload: Selling securities removes money from circulation, decreasing the money supply.

questions.statement: A lower reserve requirement allows commercial banks to lend out more money.
questions.payload: Correct. Less money locked in reserves means more available for lending, expanding the money supply.

questions.statement: Quantitative easing involves the Central Bank raising interest rates to stimulate growth.
questions.payload: QE means buying large amounts of assets to inject money into the economy — it does not involve raising rates.

questions.statement: An expansionary monetary policy is used to fight inflation.
questions.payload: Expansionary policy increases money supply and is used to stimulate growth — not fight inflation. Contractionary policy fights inflation.

questions.statement: When the Central Bank buys government bonds, money supply increases.
questions.payload: Correct. Buying bonds injects money into the banking system, expanding the money supply.

questions.statement: Raising interest rates tends to reduce consumer spending.
questions.payload: Correct. Higher rates make borrowing more costly, discouraging spending and cooling the economy.

questions.statement: The money multiplier effect means that an initial deposit creates multiple times that amount in new deposits.
questions.payload: Correct. Through repeated lending, an initial deposit is multiplied across the banking system.

questions.statement: High inflation typically causes the Central Bank to lower interest rates.
questions.payload: The Central Bank raises rates to reduce inflation by making borrowing more expensive and slowing spending.

questions.statement: Contractionary monetary policy increases the money supply.
questions.payload: Contractionary policy reduces the money supply — through higher rates or selling bonds — to slow inflation.

questions.statement: The discount rate is the interest rate commercial banks charge each other for overnight loans.
questions.payload: That's the federal funds rate. The discount rate is what the Central Bank charges commercial banks directly.

questions.statement: A central bank increasing the reserve requirement is a contractionary policy.
questions.payload: Correct. A higher reserve requirement means banks can lend less, reducing the money supply.

questions.statement: Open market operations are only effective for increasing the money supply.
questions.payload: OMOs work in both directions: buying bonds expands supply, selling bonds contracts it.

questions.statement: Low interest rates generally encourage investment by firms.
questions.payload: Correct. Cheaper borrowing costs make it easier for firms to finance projects and expand.

questions.statement: Monetary policy operates with an immediate effect on the economy.
questions.payload: Monetary policy has a time lag — changes in interest rates take months to fully filter through to spending and inflation.

questions.statement: When interest rates fall, the value of existing bonds typically rises.
questions.payload: Correct. Bond prices and interest rates move inversely — lower rates make existing higher-yield bonds more attractive.
